policy_statement: Italy’s Budget Law for 2018 obliges public bodies to redevelop public lighting systems by 31 December 2023, achieving at least a 50% reduction in electricity consumption relative to average 2015–2016.
mechanism_description: Public bodies are obliged to redevelop public lighting systems to reduce electricity consumption, lowering energy demand and associated power-sector emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Same service (public lighting) → ↓ electricity input → ↓ fossil generation → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Municipal/public authorities retrofit or replace inefficient street/public lighting (e.g., LEDs, controls, optimized luminaires) and/or redesign networks to deliver set illumination with less electricity. (2) Process: Higher lighting efficacy and smarter operation reduce kWh demand; the grid supplies less electricity, reducing dispatch from marginal generators (often fossil). (3) Outcome: Lower CO₂e emissions from power generation (and often reduced peak load), with ancillary benefits like lower operating costs.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced grid electricity consumption for public lighting, leading to lower power-sector GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: delivering the same public lighting service with less electricity, which reduces upstream generation and emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while the ultimate benefit is mitigation (lower emissions), the mechanism operates first and foremost through end-use energy efficiency (reduced energy input per service), which fits Resource Efficiency as the primary category for all such policies.  
3) Edge cases applied: if electricity is fully renewable, emissions reductions may be small, but the mechanism still reduces energy input and system costs; rebound effects are limited but possible if lighting levels are increased—still primarily an efficiency mechanism.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: any policy that upgrades public lighting efficiency to reduce electricity use shares the same dominant “same service → less energy” pathway regardless of jurisdiction or technology choice.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., specific technology, financing model, grid carbon intensity assumptions) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If upgrades are paired with higher illumination levels, expanded coverage, or decorative lighting, total kWh (and thus emissions) reductions could be smaller or negated; classification remains Resource Efficiency because the core mechanism is improved efficiency of a service. If explicitly framed as decarbonizing supply (e.g., solar-powered standalone lights), the mechanism would differ and could shift category.
key_indicators: “public bodies are obliged”; “redevelop public lighting systems”; “by 31 December 2023”; “guaranteeing a reduction in electricity consumption of at least 50%”; “in relation to the average consumption 2015-2016”
co_benefits: Reduced municipal operating costs; potential improved lighting quality/safety; reduced peak load/air pollution from power generation
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a regulatory mandate, but the defining feature is the quantified required outcome (≥50% reduction), which aligns best with a performance standard.
